title: Working with the part that cannot accept compliments
author: Kalesh

htmlContent:
Why your whole body tenses when someone says something nice

I know this moment. Someone looks at you and says something kind. "You did a good job." "I really appreciate you." "You look nice today." And instead of feeling warm, you feel something closer to panic. Your chest tightens. Your stomach drops. You want to disappear into the floor.

Here's the thing. This isn't vanity or pride or some character flaw. This is a protective part of you that has been doing its job for a very long time. Maybe your whole life. And it's terrified.

I remember the first time a therapist told me to just "accept the compliment gracefully." I nodded. I smiled. And inside I was screaming. Because that part of me knew something she didn't. It knew that accepting kindness had consequences. Real ones.

Let's look at what's actually happening in your nervous system when someone offers you a compliment. Stephen Porges, the researcher who developed Polyvagal Theory, would say your body is scanning for safety. And a compliment? That's not registering as safe. It's registering as a threat. Because your system has learned that praise is often followed by something painful. Disappointment. Betrayal. The other shoe dropping.

So that part of you that flinches? It's not broken. It's not arrogant. It's trying to protect you from the crash that always seems to come after the high. Stay with me here.

Think about what happened when you were young. Maybe praise was rare, and when it came, it came with strings attached. "You're so good at cleaning up. Now do the whole house." Or maybe praise was followed by criticism. "That's great, but you could have done better." Or maybe the people who praised you were the same people who hurt you. And accepting their kindness felt like betrayal of your own pain.

That part of you learned a survival strategy. Don't accept the compliment. Deflect it. Minimize it. Change the subject. Make yourself small. Because small was safe. Invisible was safe. Unremarkable was safe.

Wild, right? That something as simple as hearing "I like your shirt" can trigger a full nervous system response. But it does. And it's not your fault.

The protector who learned to deflect

I want you to meet this part. Not to fix it. Not to argue with it. Just to meet it.

This part has a job. Its job is to keep you from getting too visible. Because in its experience, visibility equals danger. When you're seen, you can be targeted. When you're praised, expectations rise. And when expectations rise, failure becomes possible. So this part keeps you low. Keeps you safe. Keeps you from ever having to experience the shame of being seen and then rejected.

Bessel van der Kolk, author of "The Body Keeps the Score," has spent decades showing us that trauma lives in the body. It's not just a memory you think about. It's a response that happens to you. When that compliment comes, your body responds before your conscious mind can catch up. The flinch. The heat in your face. The urge to run. That's not you being difficult. That's your body doing what it learned to do to survive.

I have a client who, every time her boss says something positive about her work, immediately lists three things she did wrong. She can't stop. She knows she's doing it. She hates that she's doing it. But that part of her is convinced that if she accepts the praise, she'll be expected to be perfect. And perfection is impossible. So she preemptively fails. She shows her flaws before anyone else can find them. It's a preemptive strike against potential disappointment.

Does that sound familiar? Maybe you do something similar. You say "It was nothing." You point out who else helped. You make a joke. You change the subject. You literally cannot let the kindness land.

That's the protector. And it's doing a hard job. It's trying to manage your emotional safety in a world that has not been safe. And honestly, it deserves some gratitude. Not dismissal. Not a lecture about how you should just accept compliments. Gratitude.

Because that part has been working overtime. For years. Maybe decades. And it's tired. But it doesn't know how to stop. Because stopping feels like letting your guard down. And letting your guard down feels like death.

For understanding how trauma fragments the self, a popular choice is Healing the Fragmented Selves of Trauma Survivors (paid link). It explains exactly how these protector parts form and why they're so stubborn. And it offers a compassionate way to work with them instead of against them.

The cost of never letting kindness in

Here's the hard part. That protector part is doing its job. But its job comes with a cost.

When you can't accept a compliment, you're not just rejecting the words. You're rejecting the connection. You're telling the other person, "I don't trust what you're offering." And that makes sense. You probably don't. Trust isn't something that came easily to you. It came with conditions. Or it came and then got ripped away.

But here's what else happens. You starve yourself. You block the only channel through which genuine connection can flow. Kindness from others is one of the few things that can actually regulate your nervous system. When someone sees you, truly sees you, and offers something warm, your body can learn to settle. But only if you let it in.

Peter Levine, the founder of Somatic Experiencing, talks about pendulation. The idea that healing happens when you can move between states of activation and settling. The compliment activates you. But if you can learn to stay with that activation, just for a moment, and let it move through you, your system can find a new settling point. A new baseline. One where kindness is allowed.

I'm not saying this is easy. It's not. The first few times you try to stay with a compliment, your body will scream at you to deflect. Your throat will close. Your mind will race. You'll want to say something, anything, to make it stop. That's normal. That's the protector doing its job. But if you can just stay for five seconds. Just five. You might notice something shift.

A structured workbook that many C-PTSD survivors find practical is The Complex PTSD Workbook (paid link). It has exercises specifically for working with these kinds of protective responses. It won't fix everything overnight. But it gives you a place to start.

Think about the relationships in your life. The ones that matter. How many of them have been damaged by your inability to receive? How many times has someone tried to love you, and you couldn't let them? Not because you didn't want to. But because the part that protects you from love was louder than the part that wants it.

Sit with that. It's uncomfortable. I know. But it's true.

How to start letting kindness land

So what do you actually do? How do you work with this part without trying to get rid of it?

First, you stop trying to get rid of it. That part is not the enemy. It's a scared child who learned that visibility equals danger. You can't bully that child into feeling safe. You have to show it, over and over, that things are different now. That you're an adult. That you can handle what comes.

Second, you practice in small doses. You don't have to accept a major compliment from your boss on a huge project. Start with something tiny. A friend says they like your shoes. Your partner says dinner was good. Someone holds the door and says "have a nice day."

Instead of deflecting, try this. Take a breath. Look at the person. Say "thank you." That's it. Just two words. You don't have to believe it. You don't have to feel it. You just have to say it. And then notice what happens in your body. Notice the urge to add something. The urge to minimize. The urge to run. Just notice it. Don't act on it.

Third, you talk to the part. Internally. When you feel that resistance rise up, you can say something like, "I know you're scared. I know you're trying to protect me. I'm not going to make you disappear. But I want to try something different. Just for a moment. And if it goes badly, I'll listen to you again."

This is parts work. Richard Schwartz, the creator of Internal Family Systems, calls it Self-energy. The compassionate, curious, calm part of you that can lead the system. That Self can talk to the protector. Not from above. Not from a place of superiority. But from a place of understanding.

A gentle introduction to parts work that many people appreciate is No Bad Parts (paid link). Schwartz walks through exactly how to have these conversations with the parts that protect you. It's not about getting rid of them. It's about helping them trust you enough to relax.

Fourth, you let yourself feel the grief. Because underneath that protector is a younger part that learned it wasn't safe to be seen. That part carries a lot of pain. Pain that was never acknowledged. Pain that was never soothed. When you start letting compliments in, that younger part might start to surface. And it might cry. It might rage. It might want to hide. Let it. Don't rush it. Don't fix it. Just be with it.

For understanding emotional neglect specifically, a helpful resource is Running on Empty (paid link). It talks about what happens when your emotional needs weren't met as a child, and how that shows up in adulthood. Including the inability to receive kindness.

The open ending

I'm not going to tell you that if you just practice enough, you'll become someone who loves compliments. That's not realistic. And it's not the goal.

The goal is to expand your window of tolerance. To make it possible for you to receive kindness without falling apart. To let the protector know that it can rest, just a little, because you're here now. You're an adult. And you can handle a few seconds of being seen.

Some days you'll fail. You'll deflect. You'll minimize. You'll change the subject. And that's okay. That's not regression. That's information. That's your system telling you that today, the threshold is lower. And that's fine. You don't have to be perfect at this. You just have to keep showing up.

The part that can't accept compliments? It's not your enemy. It's your guardian. It kept you safe when you needed it. And now, slowly, gently, you can show it a new way. A way where being seen doesn't have to hurt. A way where kindness can land, just for a moment, before the fear rushes back in.

And maybe that moment is enough. Maybe that moment, repeated a thousand times, becomes a new pattern. A new way of being in the world. Not one where you're invulnerable. But one where you're a little more open. A little more alive. A little more willing to let someone see you.

That's not weakness. That's courage. The courage to let the protector rest, just for a moment, and trust that you can handle what comes next.

Healing Journey Resources

  • The Complex PTSD Workbook (paid link) - A structured workbook with practical exercises for C-PTSD survivors
  • Healing the Fragmented Selves of Trauma Survivors (paid link) - Understanding how trauma fragments the self and how to work with protective parts
  • Running on Empty (paid link) - Understanding emotional neglect and its effects on adult relationships
  • No Bad Parts (paid link) - A gentle introduction to Internal Family Systems and working with all parts of yourself
